data.title: Vale: Cost Discipline Is Back, And The Buy Window Is Open
data.description:
Vale S.A.'s (VALE) investment case today focuses on its high correlation between iron ore price dynamics and how the company manages to measure the return on cost predictability—most clearly demonstrated by the direct operating cost to produce a ton of iron ore.

Considering that this is the driver that Vale has control over, in recent quarters, the company has shown excellent advances in C1/t, with a recent highlight in 3Q25, with values falling quarter on quarter, indicating that it is feasible to achieve its guidance, and potentially above what the market anticipated.

In the short term, the sentiment behind a rise in the price of iron ore is supported by a U.S.-China tariff trade scenario and also seasonal factors that keep the price of the commodity supported above US$100/t with some respite—even though medium-term fundamentals still point to weaker Chinese demand and rising global supply.

Even so, in my previous coverage, I had highlighted my bullish stance on Vale for 2H25 based on (1) the decrease in commitments in the case of the Mariana tragedy; (2) the consequent strengthening of a more robust FCF yield over the last two years at least; and (3) a better trend of shareholder returns going forward.

Updating this view, the 3Q25 results and the latest global developments only add credibility to the bullish thesis, with Vale possibly reaching an FCF yield close to ~14% by 2025 and considering cost control and a high iron ore price, collaborative with the export thesis.

Cost Predictability Returns as Vale's Most Reliable Bull Catalyst

The main point here that I want to highlight in Vale's bull case, reinforced in 3Q25, has to do with the return of cost predictability (C1/t) and the proof that the Brazilian company can operate more efficiently and more stably than the market expected.

Providing some context earlier, the market's biggest structural fear about Vale in recent years was not the price of ore, China, or CapEx (structural headwinds), but unanchored operating costs.

When C1/t seemed to spiral out of control due to the effects of rainfall, logistical disruptions, maintenance, inventories, and S11D swings, the market clearly began to treat Vale's operational guidance with deep skepticism—along with incorporating the dynamics of the iron ore price.

And now, in 3Q25, there are three things happening at the same time, which are:

(1) C1/t came in at US$20.7/t, which represents a level of -6.8% QoQ and flat YoY, arguably below what the market estimated and breaking the argument of lack of cost control; (2) the 2025 guidance of US$20.5-22/t has become totally feasible, unlocking confidence by showing predictability in cost and consequently less structural volatility; and (3) all-in cost falling and freight surprising positively, which shows structural dilution via volumes and total cost converging to a globally competitive level.

When we compare the iron ore big three, currently Vale with C1/t (ex-third-party purchases) at US$20.7/t reported in 3Q25 vs. BHP Group Limited's (BHP) US$17.29/t (WAIO—Pilbara operations FY2025) and Rio Tinto Group's (RIO) US$24.3/t (Pilbara iron ore unit cash cost 1H25), we can see Vale sitting between BHP and Rio which I consider very solid based on (1) higher moisture content, (2) logistics complexity, and (3) more variable seasonality.

This point alone supports a bull case, considering that the margin consists of the realized price minus the cost. Considering that iron ore is volatile and out of the company's control, the only structurally defensible driver is cost. And the sharp rise since July from ~$92 to currently ~$103.5 amplifies the re-rating.

As there is a very high correlation between Vale's price performance and spot iron ore, as the graph below shows, the company's gross margins follow the trend of the ore. And with a stable or falling cost scenario, every US$1 of ore becomes more margin. The trend of C1/t converging towards the lower end of guidance, in my view, reinforces the cost improvement as a valuation multiplier.

Furthermore, copper and nickel costs are also structurally positive for Vale. Copper all-in cost fell 17% YoY, as gold (a by-product credit), which has been on the rise due to the global risk scenario, helps to greatly reduce reported cash. As for nickel, the all-in cost was revised downwards (~7% reduction in guidance) with a more efficient operation, contributing to a real and non-seasonal improvement.

Tariff Truce and Seasonality Lift

At the end of October, the announcement of the one-year U.S.-China trade agreement with the reduction of average tariffs to 47% (-10 p.p. vs. previously) in addition to the temporary removal of restrictions on exports of rare earth metals. The idea is that this may have been a turning point in the picture of geopolitical tension, which had been adding risk to global cyclical assets, especially commodities linked to China's economy—in the very specific case of Vale.

The improvement in sentiment can be considered (1) a reduction in trade uncertainty that could unlock industrial investment plans for China; and (2) a repricing of risk for emerging exporters, increasing the flow of foreign capital.

In this situation, the tendency is for Vale to benefit from the beginning of a process of return due to the removal of the overhang, although it won't change the physical demand for ore, but it will reduce the perceived risk and avoid sharp falls.

Between 4Q25 and 1Q26, I believe that ore also tends to hold the US$100/t+ levels in the short term, considering two 100% seasonal factors: (1) Chinese restocking before the lunar new year, as steel mills tend to increase purchases between November and January—including this being a recurring movement in the last decade; (2) monsoons and rain season in Brazil, reducing the production of shipments, pressuring futures prices—DCE Jan/26 already at US$110/t being a premium of ~3% vs. spot.

While these two points tend to keep mining "artificially" sustained, fundamentally, mining tends to fall in 2026—and that's why sustaining the low C1/t is so important.

To begin with, (1) Chinese steelmakers' margins are negative, with data showing hot-rolled coil sales margins falling to breakeven levels from ~US$7-14/mt, the biggest sign of weak demand; (2) quality spread compression, showing premiums falling from US$19 to US$13-15, another sign that mining demand is cooling; (3) China's FAI (Fixed Asset Investment) accumulated in 9M25 of -0.5% YoY turning negative vs. +0.5% 8M25, something rare in China and showing that real estate has slowed down—a sector that demands 30-40% of iron ore consumption. Within the accumulated FAI, real estate remains in deep contraction (-9%/-10% YoY), while infrastructure is only growing +4%-5% YoY, losing momentum close to the high-single digits of recent years.

I believe that it makes sense here to consider from a macro and micro perspective that (1) the U.S.- China agreement will tend to boost VALE in 4Q25 and potentially 1Q26, in my view; (2) the November-January seasonality of restocking and monsoons tends to provide welcome temporary support for iron ore prices; (3) weak Chinese demand plus a potentially larger global supply could be a headwind for the course of 2026; (4) the micro (Vale) is sustained by well-done operation synergies, with C1/t cost stable, all-in cost falling and FCF remaining strong, opening up space for extraordinary dividends even in more timid iron ore scenarios.

Brumadinho and Samarco Are Becoming Contained Events, Supporting FCF and Dividends

At the end of 3Q25, Vale still showed that it carries US$4.2 billion in remaining provisions for Brumadinho + Samarco. Since 2019, the realized cash out has been US$7.8 billion for Brumadinho and R$69.9 billion (~US$14 billion) for Samarco. Now, the programmed future cash out for both, in expanded net debt, is (1) 2026: US$1.3 billion; (2) 2027: US$1.1 billion; (3) Brumadinho 2028-2031: US$0.2 billion/year and Samarco 2028-2030: US$0.3-0.4 billion/year, and then 2031-2043: US$ 0..3 billion/year.

Add all this to the decharacterization (out of expanded net debt) in 2026 of US$0.1 billion and 2027-2035 of ~ US$0.24 billion/year, and Vale will have between US$0.5 billion and US$1.4 billion/year of Brumadinho/Samarco obligations over the next decade.

So here, I want to point out that Brumadinho and Samarco are increasingly becoming "non-events", considering that the net debt of US$16.6 billion falling 5% YoY generates a trigger for extraordinary dividends when it reaches US$15 billion, and especially with the bulk of the cash-out having already passed between 2019 and 2025 with ~75% of the total provisions already settled, bringing better visibility and lower tail risk.

On top of this, FCF remains very robust.

When we look at Vale's FCF in 3Q25, coming in at US$1.56 billion (3x higher YoY), even with Brumadinho and Samarco consuming US$306 million and US$970 million, respectively, Vale still managed to generate US$2.58 billion in total FCF, showing an operation strong enough to absorb Brumadinho/Samarco without destroying cash generation.

Considering iron ore at ~US$100-110/t and C1/t ~US$20-21/t, Vale should deliver an FCF yield close to ~14% in 2025E and potentially ~15% in 2026E, even assuming an annual drain of ~US$1 billion. If 2026 turns out to be a fundamentally more discounted iron ore scenario, say ~US$85/t, the tendency is for a FCF yield of around ~4% to ~8%, which could be very similar to the current level, which tends to sustain a dividend yield of ~6% to ~8%.

In a base case, considering iron ore in 2026 at (US$90-100/t), a coherent FCF yield would be 8-11%, similar to 2023 levels, in addition to 60-80% of FCF as dividends in the long term, translating into a mid-term yield of ~6-9%.

Risks and Final Remarks

Given that Vale's fundamentals and price performance are highly dependent on trends in iron ore demand and supply, it's up to the company to do its homework, which is basically to be as efficient as possible in its cost structure.

The 3Q25 results showed an excellent advance in C1/t, making the annual guidance feasible and reiterating an increasingly competitive position versus global peers that suggests room for a re-rating.

On the cash flow side, there are risks in terms of the still heavy provisions for Brumadinho and Samarco, which could divert cash and affect dividends, as well as the political and judicial risk of revising or extending these provisions.

In addition to these risks, the combination of mining below US$100/t in 2026 and fixed bonds could create some tightness, compressing the FCF yield and preventing a full re-rating.

However, I would say that Vale is experiencing excellent momentum under the current circumstances, and in the short to medium term, with supportive trends in the commodity central to the thesis, Vale remains poised to outperform, making it an appropriate time to go long on the thesis, warranting a Buy until iron ore prices's weak signs prove otherwise.

data.title: Vanadium Miners News For The Month Of November 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the Vanadium miners news.

November saw China spot vanadium pentoxide prices flat and ferrovanadium prices slightly higher for the past month.

Vanadium uses

Vanadium is traditionally used to harden steel. Chinese rebar standards are requiring more vanadium. Also Vanadium Flow Batteries [VRFBs] are becoming increasingly popular especially for commercial energy storage, most notably in China. Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] is used in VRFBs and Ferrovanadium [FeV] is used in the steel industry.

Vanadium spot price history

China V2O5 prices were flat the last month. The Shanghai Metal Market price on November 24 was US$9,077/t (VAT excluded).

Europe & China Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] Flake 98% one year price chart (not updated this month) - Europe = ~USD 4.19/lb, China = USD 4.12/lb (or US$9,078/mt )

As of November 24, 2025, Argus Media reports the China Ferro-vanadium (80%V) FOB price at US$24.00/kg, slightly higher for the past month.

Europe and China Ferrovanadium [FeV] 80% one year price chart (not updated this month) - Europe = USD 24.30/kg, China = USD 24.00/kg

Vanadium demand versus supply

An April 2021 Wood Mackenzie report stated:

Global energy storage deployment surged a remarkable 62% in 2020, with 5 GW/9 GWh of new capacity added. This brought the total energy storage market to more than 27 GWh. Furthermore, we expect the global (energy storage) market to grow 27-fold by 2030.

ResearchAndMarkets forecasts the global VRFB market to grow from US$298m in 2023 to US$921m in 2028, at a CAGR of 20.5% (as of January 2023) (source)

Global VRFB forecast growth by region 2022-2031

Large scale global deployments of VRFB's are becoming more common - chart sourced as of April 2025 (source)

VRFB installations are rapidly appearing globally, notably in China (source)

Vanadium (8.5x) & Lithium (10x) demand forecast to surge the most under the IEA Net Zero Emission scenario (source)

Vanadium market news

On November 17 Vanitec reported:

Enerflow signs 1.2 GWh long-duration energy storage cooperation in Australia. Enerflow Technology Co., Ltd. (Enerflow) has entered into a strategic cooperation agreement with Australian firm JENMI INVESTMENTS PTY LTD to jointly develop the market for vanadium flow battery energy storage systems and electrolyte in Australia, including a planned 350 MW / 1,200 MWh large-scale energy storage project.

On November 17 Vanitec reported:

0.25 MW / 2 MWh Vanadium Flow Battery energy storage system at Sichuan investment building enters commercial operation.

On November 25 Discovery Alert reported:

Kalgoorlie’s Vanadium Battery Project: $150 million energy storage initiative...This ambitious project encompasses domestic vanadium extraction, electrolyte manufacturing, and grid-scale battery deployment in Western Australia's Goldfields region...The planned 50-megawatt, 500-megawatt-hour vanadium flow battery system represents significant infrastructure development for Australia's energy storage sector. This scale of deployment, described by government officials as the largest vanadium battery installation in the country...The 10-hour discharge capability positions this system for applications requiring extended energy delivery periods. Unlike lithium-ion batteries typically designed for 1-4 hour discharge durations, vanadium flow batteries excel in applications requiring sustained power output over longer timeframes. This makes them particularly suitable for renewable energy firming and grid stabilisation services...The Western Australian Government's commitment includes $150 million in funding support for the vanadium battery installation project...The Expression of Interest process, operating until January 30, 2026, aims to identify existing market capabilities and evaluate detailed business cases from potential project partners.

Vanadium miner news

Vanadium producers

Glencore [LSX:GLEN] [HK:805] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

Glencore is a large vanadium producer, but vanadium production represents only a small portion of their revenue.

On November 19 Mining Weekly reported:

Glencore-Merafe venture to start retrenchments within weeks, idle two of its three ferrochrome smelters. The Glencore-Merafe Chrome Venture has confirmed that it plans to place its Wonderkop and Boshoek smelters, in South Africa's North West province, on care and maintenance and to start retrenching employees at these operations within weeks.

AMG Critical Materials N.V. [NA:AMG] [GR:ADG] (OTCPK:AMVMF) ("AMG")

AMG Vanadium is a leading provider of products and services for the metals, manufacturing, refinery and petrochemical industries. You can read more about AMG Vanadium here.

On November 5 AMG announced:

AMG reports strong Third Quarter 2025 results...third quarter 2025 revenue of $435 million, a 22% increase compared to the third quarter 2024 revenue of $356 million. AMG achieved an adjusted EBITDA of $64 million, a 58% increase compared to the third quarter of 2024 adjusted EBITDA of $40 million, driven primarily by the AMG Technologies segment...The current low-price environment in our two main products, lithium and vanadium, continues to constrain profitability and cash generation...

AMG Vanadium B.V.

  • SARBV’s development with Advanced Circular Materials Company (ACMC) “Supercenter” phase 1 project in Saudi Arabia remains on schedule in the detailed engineering phase.

Largo Inc. [TSX:LGO] [GR:LR81] (LGORF)(NASDAQ:LGO)

Largo Inc. is a pure-play vanadium pentoxide producer from their Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil as well as a producer of VRFBs.

On November 12 Largo announced:

Largo reports Third Quarter 2025 Financial results.

  • Revenues of $33.3 million ($32.3 million from vanadium sales and $1.0 million from ilmenite sales) in Q3 2025...
  • Revenues per lb sold3 of V2O5 equivalent of $6.06 in Q3 2025 vs. $6.28 in Q3 2024.
  • Adjusted cash operating costs excluding royalties per pound sold3 of $3.03 in Q3 2025...
  • Mining operations adjusted EBITDA3 of $4.0 million in Q3 2025 vs. $2.4 million in Q3 2024.
  • Net loss before tax of $10.4 million (including $3.7 million in non-recurring items) in Q3 2025...
  • Basic loss per share of $0.57 in Q3 2025 vs. basic loss per share of $0.16 in Q3 2024...
  • Production of 2,636 tonnes (5.8 million lbs1) of V2O5 in Q3 2025 vs. 3,072 tonnes in Q3 2024...

On November 13 Largo announced: "Leadership Transition: J. Alberto Arias and Daniel Tellechea to become Co-CEOs and Diogo Silva Promoted to Become CFO."

Energy Fuels Inc. [TSX:EFR] (UUUU)

Energy Fuels state they are "the No. 1 uranium producer in the U.S. with a market-leading portfolio", as well as being a small vanadium producer.

On November 3 Energy Fuels announced:

Q3-2025 Results...Reduced Net Loss of $16.7 million compared to Q2 2025: During Q3-2025, the Company incurred a net loss of $16.7 million, or $0.07 per common share, which is an improvement compared to a net loss of $21.8 million, or $0.10 per common share during Q2-2025, while at the same time advancing the Company's development projects and prepping the Company's White Mesa Mill (the "Mill") for a conventional uranium ore processing run that commenced in early October 2025...Inventory...The Company also held 905,000 pounds of finished vanadium ("V2O5").

Ferro-Alloy Resources [LON:FAR]

FAR is operating the giant Balasausqandiq vanadium project in Kyzylordinskaya Oblast of southern Kazakhstan. FAR stated in May 2025: "On a forward looking basis, process plant operations will be conducted only when suitably profitable raw materials are available."

On November 4 Ferro-Alloy Resources announced:

Project finance expression of interest, front end engineering and design terms agreed and substantial capital cost savings identified... Balasausqandiq vanadium project (the "Project") in Southern Kazakhstan...

  • CC6 have provided an indicative construction cost estimate of US$261m (excluding the relatively minor costs of the equipment relating to uranium and molybdenum sorption), that cuts the funding required to get into production to US$311.9m, a 40% reduction compared to the Feasibility Study.
  • Corresponding enhanced Project internal rate of return ("IRR") of 31% (unlevered, post-tax) and net present value ("NPV") of US$931.6m.
  • CC6 has received a US$221.8m non-binding conditional loan offer from the Bank of Communication (Hubei) Branch ("BOC Bank"), equating to 85% of the CC6 scope of work
  • Potential access to Sinosure, the Chinese insurance and export credit scheme, substantially lowering financing costs.

Western Uranium & Vanadium Corp. (OTCQX:WSTRF)

Western Uranium & Vanadium Corp. own the Sunday Mine Complex which is an advanced stage mine property consisting of five interconnected underground mines in Colorado, USA.

No news for the month.

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. [TSXV:VRB][GR:NWN] (OTCPK:VRBFF)

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. 100% owns the Lac Dore Vanadium-Iron-Titanium Project in Quebec Canada. The Company is looking to take a vertically integrated approach and is also developing leading process technologies ‘VanadiumCorp-Electrochem Processing Technology’ and "Electrochem globally patented Electrowinning" technology.

On November 25 VanadiumCorp. announced: "VanadiumCorp gets conditional approval closes first tranche of Flow-Through Financing."

Vanadium developers

Vanadium Resources Limited [ASX:VR8] [GR:TR3]

Vanadium Resources is a junior exploration company established with the purpose of exploring and developing gold zinc, lead, copper and other mineral opportunities. Vanadium Resources owns 86.49% of a globally significant vanadium project, the Steelpoortdrift [SPD] Project, in Gauteng Province, South Africa.

On October 31 Vanadium Resources announced:

Quarterly Activities Report - September 2025...During the Quarter, VR8’s near term cash flow strategy continued to advance, with commercial and technical preparations undertaken to enable the production of vanadium-rich magnetite direct shipping ore (“DSO”)...DSO offtake agreement was signed with China Precious Asia Limited (“CPAL”) for 100ktpm. The agreement subsequently lapsed after receipt of multiple competing proposals from credible trading houses and end-users for DSO.

Australian Vanadium [ASX:AVL] [GR:JT71]

Australian Vanadium is an emerging vanadium producer focused on their Australian Vanadium Project in Western Australia. VSUN Energy was launched by AVL in 2016 to target the energy storage market for vanadium redox flow batteries [VRFBs].

On October 31 Australian Vanadium announced:

Quarterly Activities Report - Period ending 30 September 2025... Australian Vanadium Project. Optimised Feasibility Study: Phase 2 of the study progressed significantly and is now approaching completion...VSUN Energy continues to pursue multiple utility-scale VFB opportunities across five Australian states, including preparatory work for potential participation in the Western Australian Government’s proposed landmark 50 MW / 10-hour (500 MWh) vanadium battery energy storage system (VBESS) in Kalgoorlie.

On November 25 Australian Vanadium announced:

EOI OPEN FOR KALGOORLIE VBESS. Australian Vanadium and VSUN Energy preparing submission for Expression of Interest. WA Government opens Expression of Interest for proponents to deliver the 50 MW / 10- hour (500 MWh) Kalgoorlie Vanadium Battery Energy Storage System by 2029 on a build-own-operate basis, backed by a $150 million funding commitment.

Richmond Vanadium Technology [ASX:RVT] ("RVT")

RVT now owns 100% of the Richmond Vanadium Project. It has a global Mineral Resource of 1.8Bt @ 0.36% Vanadium Pentoxide (V2O5).

On November 14 RVT announced:

2025 AGM Chair's Address to Shareholders...restructuring has allowed us to focus resources on advancing the Environmental Impact Statement (EIS), Bankable Feasibility Study (BFS), and metallurgical test work program — the core pillars of our next growth phase.

Phenom Resources Corp. [TSXV:PHNM] (OTCQX:PHNMF) (formerly First Vanadium Corp.)

The Carlin Gold-Vanadium Property hosts one of North America’s largest richest primary vanadium deposits, located in Nevada. Its West Jerome project targets a large scale high grade copper and zinc deposit in Arizona. Carlin has a Historic Inferred Resource 28Mt at 0.525% V2O5 (2010 SRK).

No news for the month.

CleanTech Vanadium Mining Corp. [TSXV:CTV] (OTCQB:CTVFF)

CleanTech Vanadium's flagship project is the Gibellini Vanadium Project in Nevada, USA. Gibellini holds 127 million pounds of measured and indicated V2O5, with 195 million pounds inferred. CleanTech aims to start the first North American vanadium mine. The Gibellini Vanadium Project has also shown potential for gold exploration.

No news for the month.

QEM Limited [ASX:QEM]

QEM Limited owns the Julia Creek Vanadium and Energy Project ("JCVEP") in North Western Queensland, Australia.

On October 31 QEM announced:

Quarterly update for the quarter ending 30 September 2025...Launch of a Strategic review of the JCVEP, with a focus on preserving capital and assessing all project and corporate opportunities.

On November 26 QEM announced:

Chairman's AGM Address...Post the financial year close, test work based on key risks identified in the Scoping Study, specifically validating key separation steps in the Feed Preparation Facility flowsheet regrettably proved unsuccessful, necessitating a slowdown in project activities and a strategic review of the JCVEP, as reported to the market on 23 September 2025. We anticipate that the strategic review will be completed late in Q1 2026.

Tivan Limited [ASX:TVN] (formerly TNG Ltd [ASX:TNG](OTCPK:TNGZF)

Tivan 100% owns Speewah Mining Pty Ltd, the owner of the Speewah Vanadium-Titanium-Iron Project and the Speewah Fluorite Project in WA. They also own the high grade Sandover Fluorite Project in Northern Territory.

On November 5 Tivan announced:

Tivan acquires highly prospective mineral concessions in Timor-Leste...The Licences cover an area of 289km 2 and are considered highly prospective for copper, cobalt and gold, and other critical minerals.

On November 17 Tivan announced: "Tivan and ETFS Capital execute binding agreements for up to $61.3 million investment in the Speewah Fluorite Project."

On November 19 Tivan announced: "Tivan progresses Feasibility Study for Speewah Fluorite Project."

Neometals [ASX:NMT] (OTCQX:NMTAY)(OTCPK:RDRUY) (OTCPK:RRSSF)

Neometals 100% own the Barrambie Titanium Vanadium Iron Project in Western Australia, but are looking at divesting the Barrambie VTM Deposit, but keeping the Barrambie Gold Project. They also own 86.1% of the Vanadium Recovery Project JV.

On November 6 Neometals announced: "Barrambie - Positive Metallurgical Sighter Test Work results...High gold recoveries achieved from tested samples using low risk, industry standard processing flowsheet."

On November 17 Neometals announced: "Finnish Vanadium Recovery Project receives €48.7 million (c. $86.8 AUD Million) Conditional Grant from Business Finland."

Graphite miners with potential vanadium projects

  • Syrah Resources [ASX:SYR] (OTCPK:SYAAF) (OTC:SRHYY)
  • NextSource Materials [TSX:NEXT]
  • DNI Metals [TSXV:DNI] [GR:DG7N](OTC:DMNKF)

Other listed vanadium juniors

  • BlackRock Metals (Private)
  • Blue Sky Uranium [TSXV:BSK] (OTCQB:BKUCF)
  • Critical Minerals Group [ASX:CMG]
  • Currie Rose Resources Inc. [TSXV:CUI]
  • Gladiator Resources [ASX:GLA]
  • Golcap Resources Corp. [CSE:GCP]
  • Golden Deeps [ASX:GED]
  • Intermin Resources [ASX:IRC]
  • Manuka Resources [ASX:MKR]
  • Maxtech Ventures [CSE:MVT]
  • Moab Minerals [ASX:MOM]
  • New Energy Minerals [ASX: NXE]
  • Pursuit Minerals [ASX:PUR]
  • Sabre Resources [ASX:SBR]
  • Santa Fe Minerals [ASX:SFM]
  • Strategic Resources [TSXV:SR] (OTCPK:SCCFF)
  • Surefire Resources [ASX:SRN]
  • Trigon Metals Inc. [TSXV:TM] (OTC:PNTZF)
  • UVRE [ASX:UVA]
  • Venus Metals [ASX:VMC]
  • Victory Metals [TSXV:VMX]
  • Viking Mines [ASX:VKA]

VRFB Companies

  • Protean Energy [ASX:POW] [GR:SHE1]
  • Enerox GmbH
  • Invinity Energy Systems (LSE:IES) (IVVGF) (OTCQX:IESVF)

Conclusion

November saw China spot vanadium pentoxide prices flat and ferrovanadium prices slightly higher for the past month. Prices remain at very low levels.

Highlights for the month include:

  • Enerflow signs 1.2 GWh long-duration energy storage cooperation in Australia.
  • 2 MWh Vanadium Flow Battery energy storage system at Sichuan investment building enters commercial operation.
  • Kalgoorlie’s Vanadium Battery Project: Energy storage initiative. Includes A$150 million in funding support from the WA government.
  • Glencore-Merafe venture to start retrenchments within weeks, idle two of its three ferrochrome smelters.
  • AMG reports strong Third Quarter 2025 results, EBITDA of $64 million, a 58% YoY increase. “Supercenter” phase 1 project in Saudi Arabia remains on schedule.
  • Largo Inc. reported a Q3 loss of US$0.57 per share. Announces new co-CEOs.
  • Energy Fuels reported reduced Net Loss of $16.7M compared to Q2 2025.
  • Ferro-Alloy Resources Project finance expression of interest, front end engineering and design terms agreed and substantial capital cost savings identified for the Balasausqandiq vanadium project in Kazakhstan.
  • Australian Vanadium subsidiary VSUN Energy to lodge a submission for participation in the Western Australian Government’s proposed landmark 500 MWh vanadium battery energy storage system (VBESS) in Kalgoorlie.
  • Neometals' Finnish Vanadium Recovery Project receives €48.7 million (c. $86.8 AUD Million) Conditional Grant from Business Finland.

As usual all comments are welcome.

data.title: TCW Emerging Markets Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance and Positioning*

The TCW Emerging Markets Income Fund, I Class (“Fund”) returned 5.50% (net) for the third quarter of 2025, outperforming the EMBI GD (benchmark) return of 4.75% by 75bps. Year-to-date through September 30, 2025, the Fund returned 11.42% (net), outperforming the benchmark return of 10.66% by 76bps.

Relative outperformance for the third quarter was driven by an overweight to high yield and security selection in both high yield and investment grade. Off-index exposure to corporates also contributed to relative performance for the period. From a country perspective, relative outperformance was driven by:

  • Overweight positioning and security selection in Senegal as it bounced off year-to-date lows on completion of the government debt audit and progress with IMF on a new financing program
  • Overweight positioning in Colombia driven by a combination of select off-index corporate exposure and sovereign exposure which benefited from the government’s recent liability management exercise
  • Underweight positioning in China which underperformed the Index and overweight positioning in Angola and Ivory Coast
  • Overweight positioning and security selection in select idiosyncratic high yield issuers including Venezuela and Ukraine
  • Underweight positioning and security selection in Argentina, where bonds have been under pressure from elevated political risk following President Milei’s defeat in the recent Buenos Aires legislative elections
  • Security selection in Morocco and Brazil, driven by select off-index corporate exposure

On the other hand, underweight positioning in Kenya, Bolivia and Bahrain hurt relative performance for the quarter. Off-index exposure in Israel, overweight positioning in Gabon, and security selection in Peru and Panama also detracted from relative performance for the period.

During the quarter, we added exposure in Colombia, Hungary, and in select idiosyncratic high yield issuers including Ukraine and Venezuela. We also added corporate exposure in Jamaica and off-index sovereign exposure in Kuwait. On the other hand, we exited our exposure in Pakistan as spreads tightened to year-to-date lows. We also reduced quasi-sovereign and corporate exposure in Mexico and Chile.

From a regional perspective, we ended the quarter with overweights in Africa, Latin America and Europe, against an underweight in Asia and the Middle East. We continue to maintain an overweight in high yield (+6.7% relative to the benchmark, 56.3% total, as of quarter end), focusing on idiosyncratic and relative value opportunities.

As of quarter end, the Fund’s largest overweights were Colombia, Romania, Senegal, Brazil and Angola against underweights in tighter trading investment grade sovereigns such as China, Malaysia, Indonesia and Philippines. We are maintaining a duration close to the benchmark, underweight by 0.38 years at the portfolio level, with an overweight in duration in the investment-grade sleeve of the portfolio (excluding cash) and short duration positioning in high yield.

Investment Environment and Outlook

Emerging Markets (EM) fixed income delivered strong results in Q3 2025, with sovereign and corporate dollar debt returning 4.75% and 3.18%, respectively, outperforming U.S. high yield, which returned 2.43%. Spreads on EM sovereign dollar debt tightened by 39bps to 284bps, near historical lows. Despite persistent uncertainty around global growth and trade policy year-to-date, EM sovereign and corporate dollar debt returned 10.66% and 7.34%, respectively, surpassing U.S. high yield’s return of 6.79%, through September 30, 2025.

Global growth appears likely to decelerate from 3.3% in 2024 to 3.2% in 2025 and further to 3.1% into 2026 per IMF’s latest forecast. (See Figure 1.) This is largely driven by the U.S. outlook as well as by China, where policy-driven momentum from the first half of 2025 is already fading. The exception to this is Europe, where fiscal stimulus and the lagged effects of ECB monetary easing should be supportive next year.

Figure 1: EM Growth to Outpace DM Growth

EM economies remain a bright spot, with forecasted growth of 4% expected to outpace Developed Markets (DM) economies by 2.5 times, even with China likely slowing in the second half due to a weak property market, fading infrastructure stimulus, and softening manufacturing investment. We foresee resilience in countries that have relatively stronger domestic fundamentals, especially for those with prudent economic policies. Asian economies ex-China should benefit from supply chain diversification and in the CEE, growth is likely to benefit from German spending on defense and infrastructure, which is anticipated to begin in 2026. In the Middle East, growth is picking up as the region’s oil producers continue to unwind production cuts from the past few years. In Latin America, growth is likely to remain moderate due to both domestic economic cycles and rising external uncertainty, but Brazil should continue to exhibit resilience and there is potential for elections in various countries to drive a shift towards more market-friendly administrations.

Inflationary pressures in EM countries have softened faster than expected during 2025, allowing for a more decisive monetary policy response relative to advanced economies - a trend likely to be extended by the U.S. Fed easing in 2025 and 2026. (See Figure 2.) Similarly, the tariff shock may have a disproportionately larger price impact in the U.S. or EU than in most EM countries. Further, EM remains uniquely positioned to take advantage of shifts in global trade dynamics from a unipolar to a multipolar world, with 42% of global trade already taking place outside of the traditional U.S./European nexus. Still, EM countries are unlikely to completely escape the anticipated deceleration in global growth, international trade, and capital flows, especially as advanced economies grapple with large public debt burdens and political divisions.

Figure 2: Disinflation Across DM and EM

Although the Trump administration has backed down from most worst-case scenario threats, new tariff ultimatums against China and Europe reinforce the perception that uncertainties can re-emerge at almost any time. This will complicate the macro outlook for both real-economy participants and financial markets. Indeed, the next 12 months are likely to be marked by elevated uncertainty as the world’s larger economies lead the shift to a more multipolar world via longer-term policy decisions that realign trade, capital flows and supply chains.

Against this challenging backdrop, global growth demonstrated remarkable resilience in the first half of 2025 as trade activity jumped in anticipation of higher U.S. tariffs. Currently, the U.S. effective tariff rate is around 17% – the highest level since the 1930s – from approximately 3% at the end of 2024. That said, this environment presents a wide range of opportunities in the 68 country EMBI index, ranging from exporters of critical materials to domestic growth stories to those countries likely to benefit from the realignment of trade flows and geopolitical alliances.

While credit spreads are at the tighter end of historical ranges, we continue to find EM valuations attractive relative to U.S. credit. With yields of 5.1-5.3%, EM investment grade sovereign and corporate bonds offer a 30 to 60 basis point premium over U.S. investment grade corporates. On the other hand, EM high yield offers even more compelling spread premiums. EM high yield sovereigns are trading 200bps wider to U.S. high yield and this spread differential is in the 57th percentile over the past decade.

EM local currency, up 16% this year, presents an attractive long-term opportunity. Despite a 9% decline, the USD remains near multi-decade highs on a real effective basis. An economic slowdown in the U.S. and potential for further Fed easing could erode its carry and growth advantage, leaving room for additional USD weakness. Furthermore, depreciation pressure may stem from an increase in USD hedge ratios by foreign investors amid continued trade policy uncertainty. While the USD is often viewed as a safe haven asset, current conditions suggest limited upside in both magnitude and duration.

Looking ahead towards 2026, we anticipate somewhat more clarity over the coming quarters around the extent and persistence of U.S. trade tariffs, additional monetary easing by major central banks (especially the Fed), and de-escalation of at least some major geopolitical flashpoints (e.g. Gaza) even if others remain unresolved (Russia-Ukraine). While policy surprises – especially in the U.S. – could well spark new episodes of (likely brief) financial stress, we maintain a moderately constructive outlook for EM over the medium term driven by looser monetary policies in major economies, a potentially weaker U.S. dollar, the redirection of global trade (with increasing diversification and intra-EM flows), the recovery of investment due to the likely realignment of supply chains and improving fundamentals in the asset class as a whole as evidenced by the positive ratio of rating upgrades to downgrades over the last couple of years.

Sincerely,

Penelope D. Foley, Group Managing Director, Emerging Markets | David I. Robbins, Group Managing Director, Emerging Markets

Performance (As of September 30, 2025)

Source: TCW, FactSet, State Street B&T

[1] The annualized since inception return for the index reflects the inception date of the TCW I Share (9/1/1996-9/30/2025), I-3 Share (8/26/2025-9/30/2025), N Share (2/27/2004-9/30/2025), and P Share (2/28/2020-9/30/2025) Funds, respectively.

[2] Since inception returns include the performance of the predecessor limited partnership for periods before the Fund’s registration became effective. The predecessor limited partnership was not registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 (“1940 Act”) and therefore, was not subject to certain investment restrictions imposed by the 1940 Act. If the limited partnership was registered under the 1940 Act, its performance may have been adversely affected.

The contractual fee waiver/ expense reimbursement will remain in place through March 1, 2026 for the I, N, & P Shares and through August 24, 2026 for the I-3 Shares, and before those dates, the investment advisor may not terminate these arrangements without approval of the Board of Directors.

The performance data presented represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Returns assume all income items are reinvested. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Performance data current to the most recent month end is available on the Fund’s website at TCW.com. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate with market conditions. The value of an investment in the Fund, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than its original purchase cost.

Important Disclosure

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. A Fund’s Prospectus and Summary Prospectus contain this and other information about the Fund. To receive a Prospectus, please call 800-241-4671 or you may download the Prospectus from the Fund’s website at tcw.com. Please read it carefully.

TCW, its officers, directors, employees or clients may have positions in securities or investments mentioned in this publication, which positions may change at any time, without notice. This material may include estimates, projections and other “forward-looking” statements. Actual events may differ substantially from those presented. TCW assumes no duty to update any such statements. All projections and estimates are based on current asset prices and are subject to change.

Investment Risks

Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk, the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk, and price volatility risk. Funds investing in bonds can lose their value as interest rates rise and an investor can lose principal. Fund share prices and returns will fluctuate with market conditions, currencies, and the economic and political climates where the investments are made. Emerging markets securities carry special risks, such as less developed or less efficient trading markets, a lack of company information, and differing auditing and legal standards. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile. The Fund’s investments denominated in foreign currencies will decline in value if the foreign currency declines in value relative to the U.S. dollar.

All investing involves risk including the potential loss of principal. Market volatility may significantly impact the value of your investments. Recent tariff announcements may add to this volatility, creating additional economic uncertainty and potentially affecting the value of certain investments. Tariffs can impact various sectors differently, leading to changes in market dynamics and investment performance.

Please see the Fund’s Prospectus for more information on these and other risks.

Index Disclosure

JP Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index (EMBI GD) – A market capitalization-weighted total return index of U.S. dollar-denominated Brady bonds, loans, and Eurobond instruments traded in emerging markets. The index is not available for direct investment; therefore its performance does not reflect a reduction for fees or expenses incurred in managing a portfolio. The securities in the index may be substantially different from those in the Fund. The comparison is presented to show the performance of emerging market debt securities during the period.

Glossary of Terms

Basis Points (bps)– one hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bond– A fixed income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically corporate or governmental). A bond could be thought of as an I.O.U. between the lender and borrower that includes the details of the loan and its payments. A bond has an end date when the principal of the loan is due to be paid to the bond owner and usually includes the terms for variable or fixed interest payments that will be made by the borrower. Bonds are used by companies, municipalities, states, and sovereign governments to finance projects and operations. Owners of bonds are debtholders, or creditors, of the issuer. Capital Flows– The movement of money for investment, trade, or business production across international borders. They can be direct investments, portfolio investments, or debt. Carry– The return obtained from holding an investment (if positive), or the cost of holding it (if negative). CEE– Central and Eastern Europe. Central Bank– A monopolized and often nationalized institution given privileged control over the production and distribution of money and credit. Corporate– Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. CPI (Consumer Price Index)– Measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services. Credit– A contractual agreement in which a borrower receives something of value now and agrees to repay the lender at a later date, generally with interest. Credit also refers to the creditworthiness or credit history of an individual or company. Currency– A generally accepted form of money, including coins and paper notes, which is issued by a government and circulated within an economy. Developed Markets (DM)– Countries that have sound, well-established economies and are therefore thought to offer safer, more stable investment opportunities than developing markets. Duration– A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. ECB (European Central Bank)– The European Central Bank is the central bank responsible for monetary policy of the European Union member countries that have adopted the euro currency. This currency union is known as the eurozone and currently includes 19 countries. The ECB’s primary objective is price stability in the euro area. Emerging Market (EM)– A country that has some characteristics of a developed market but is not a developed market. This includes countries that may be developed markets in the future or were in the past. EMBI– Emerging Market Bond Index. EMFX– Emerging Markets currencies. European Union (EU)– A group of European countries that participates in the world economy as one economic unit and operates under one official currency, the euro. Expense Ratio – A measure of what it costs an investment company to operate a mutual fund. Federal Reserve (the Fed)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hedge Ratio– A hedge ratio is a financial metric used in risk management to determine the size of a hedging instrument needed to offset the risk of an underlying asset. High Yield– A bond that is rated below investment grade. Idiosyncratic Risk– Specific to a security or an asset class and risk that is uncorrelated with the overall market risk. Idiosyncratic risk can be substantially mitigated or eliminated from a portfolio through diversification. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. International Monetary Fund (IMF)– An international organization created for the purpose of promoting global monetary and exchange rate stability, facilitating the expansion and balanced growth of international trade and assisting in the establishment of a multilateral system of payments for current transactions. Intra-EM Glows– Refers to the movement of capital and trade between emerging markets, as opposed to flows into or out of these markets from developed economies. This includes both financial flows like investments and trade flows, such as goods and services. Investment Grade (IG)– A bond with a rating of Baa or higher from Moody’s Investors Service, and a rating of BBB or higher from Standard & Poor’s. JP Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index (EMBI GD)– A market capitalization-weighted total return index of U.S. dollar-denominated Brady bonds, loans, and Eurobond instruments traded in emerging markets. Local Currency– The most common form of currency used in a country. This usually encompasses the national currency of the country. Macroeconomic– Relating to the branch of economics concerned with large-scale or general economic factors, such as interest rates and national productivity. Monetary Easing- A central bank’s expansionary policy to stimulate a sluggish economy by increasing the money supply and lowering interest rates. Monetary Policy– The actions of a central bank, currency board or other regulatory committee that determine the size and rate of growth of the money supply, which in turn affects interest rates. Multipolar– A multipolar system describes a global economy shifting from the dominance of a single power (like the U.S. dollar) to a more balanced framework where multiple economic centers, currencies, and markets have significant influence. This transition involves the rise of emerging markets and new trading blocs, leading to more competition, strategic shifts in investment, and a greater need for global companies and investors to adapt to a less dollar-centric world. Mutual Funds– An investment vehicle that is made up of a pool of funds collected from many investors for the purpose of investing in securities such as stocks, bonds, money-market instruments and similar assets. Mutual funds are operated by money managers, who invest the fund’s capital and attempt to produce capital gains and income for the fund’s investors. A mutual fund’s portfolio is structured and maintained to match the investment objectives stated in its prospectus. Off-Index– Securities that are not part of the Fund’s benchmark index. Outperform– Outperform is when an investment is expected to perform better than the return generated by a particular index or the overall market. Since the performance of many investments is compared to a benchmark index, outperform refers to generating a higher return than a particular benchmark over time. Outperform also refers to an analyst’s rating on a security, and outperform is a better rating than neutral and worse than a strong buy recommendation. Overweight– A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. Quasi-sovereigns– A public sector entity or corporation majority-owned or otherwise controlled by a sovereign government. Debt issued by a Quasi-sovereign entity can be explicitly or implicitly guaranteed by the sovereign. Relative Value– A method of determining an asset’s worth that takes into account the value of similar assets. S&P 500 Index (Standard & Poor's 500 Index)– A market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. Sovereign– A national government within a given country. Spread– The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Stimulus– Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Tariff– A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Tighten/Tightening– Short for tight monetary policy. A situation in which a central bank enacts relatively high target interest rates to lower the available of credit. Effectively “tightening” the supply of credit. Total Return– The rate of return on a security, including income from dividends and interest, as well as appreciation or depreciation in the price of the security, over a given time period of time. Trade Flows– Referring to the international exchange of goods and services between countries, which is intrinsically linked to the flow of financial capital Trade Policy– Refers to the regulations and agreements that control imports and exports to foreign countries. Underperform– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Unipolar– A system characterized by a single dominant financial order where one country has unparalleled influence and the ability to use financial tools, such as sanctions, to enforce its policies. USD (United States Dollar)– Abbreviated as USD, the dollar is the currency for the United States of America. U.S./European Nexus– Refers to the shared economic and political values which are the foundation of the transatlantic relationship. Valuations– The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day-to-day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value.

The TCW Funds are distributed by TCW Funds Distributors LLC
